title: The Counter-UAS Certification Bottleneck
gist: Congress's Safer Skies Act extended counter-drone authority to state and local law enforcement but conditioned it on certification through a single federal 'national schoolhouse' (the FBI's Huntsville center) projected to certify only ~60 officers by June 2026 against 18,500+ agencies—a structural bottleneck that leaves officers first on scene but unable to act, fixable only by shifting to a distributed, federally accredited state-execution training model.

insights.claim: The Safer Skies Act conditions state and local counter-UAS authority on certification through a single 'national schoolhouse'—the FBI's National Counter-UAS Training Center in Huntsville—designated as the 'sole certifying authority,' creating a centralized chokepoint on the pace at which jurisdictions can act.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: A single federal pipeline gates the entire counter-drone rollout—a structural supply constraint that delays the addressable counter-UAS market for years.
insights.quote: More than 18,500 state, local, and correctional agencies depend on a single federal schoolhouse
insights.locator: But the statute conditions the exercise of that authority

insights.claim: The FBI schoolhouse graduated its first class of ~12 officers in November 2025 and targets only ~60 state, local, tribal, and territorial officers by June 2026, against approximately 17,500 law enforcement agencies plus 1,000+ correctional facilities—over 18,500 entities each needing multiple operators.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: Quantifies the gap: ~60 certified officers vs. 18,500+ agencies means nationwide coverage is measured in years, leaving a sustained defensive vacuum.
insights.quote: the FBI has targeted training for only about 60 state, local, tribal, and territorial officers by June 2026
insights.locator: Supply: The Training Model and Its Limits

insights.claim: In jurisdictions without certified personnel, officers may be first on scene but unable to exercise counter-UAS authorities, narrowing response to observation and federal coordination—while threats like contraband drops, infrastructure surveillance, and incursions over mass gatherings resolve in minutes.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: The certification gap translates directly into unaddressed drone incidents; near-term, the authority exists on paper but cannot be operationalized.
insights.quote: officers will continue arriving at drone incidents they cannot lawfully resolve

insights.claim: Correctional agencies across multiple states have documented drones delivering narcotics, weapons, and cell phones into secure facilities, with inmates overdosing within hours of confirmed incursions, and NFL stadiums under Temporary Flight Restrictions have had drone incursions trigger temporary suspensions of play.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: Concrete, recurring harm at prisons and stadiums establishes immediate demand for deployable counter-UAS capability.
insights.quote: In several cases, inmates have overdosed within hours of a confirmed incursion.
insights.locator: For example, correctional agencies across multiple states

themes.theme: Funding tied to the same bottleneck

insights.claim: FEMA's Counter-UAS Grant Program conditions $500 million in federal equipment funding on the same certification requirement administered through the single national schoolhouse, so equipment buildout nationwide is tied to the constrained certification pipeline.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: A $500M equipment funding stream is gated by the certification bottleneck—procurement of counter-UAS gear cannot scale faster than officers get certified.
insights.quote: $500 million in federal equipment funding is conditioned on the same certification requirement
insights.locator: Under the Federal Emergency Management Agency's Counter-UAS Grant Program

insights.claim: The author's proposed fix is a train-the-trainer model: instructors certified through the federal pipeline then deliver federally approved, federally accredited training at the state level, multiplying throughput while federal authorities retain control over standards, curriculum, and accreditation.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: A distributed certification model is the lever that would unlock the counter-UAS market at scale; watch for legislative/regulatory moves toward state-executed training.
insights.quote: A state-executed model scales training across jurisdictions by multiplying certified instructors, significantly increasing throughput while keeping standards uniform.

insights.claim: FEMA's National Incident Management System proves federally prescribed standards can be delivered through distributed state execution across thousands of jurisdictions without sacrificing consistency, refuting the objection that distributed delivery risks instructor drift.
insights.speaker: Philip W. Rohlfing
insights.so_what: There is a working precedent for the proposed fix, raising the likelihood that a distributed counter-UAS training model is adopted.
insights.quote: The Federal Emergency Management Agency's National Incident Management System demonstrates that federally prescribed standards are deliverable through distributed state execution without sacrificing consistency.
insights.locator: Critics may argue that distributed delivery risks inconsistent outcomes
